narrative:
La incertidumbre geopolítica derivada del conflicto entre Estados Unidos e Irán se ha convertido en el motor principal que condiciona la percepción de riesgo en los mercados globales. El presidente Trump ha impuesto un plazo límite para el 7 de abril de 2026 a las 8pm ET, exigiendo a Irán la reapertura del Estrecho de Hormuz y un alto el fuego, demandas que han sido rechazadas por Teherán. Esta tensión se agrava con el apoyo ruso a Irán en materia de inteligencia y ciberataques, mientras mediadores internacionales intentan facilitar un acuerdo. La relevancia de este driver radica en que define un escenario de alta incertidumbre con potenciales impactos directos sobre la estabilidad regional, los precios energéticos y la dinámica global de riesgo.

Frente a este contexto, los mercados muestran una volatilidad elevada, reflejando la cautela ante la proximidad del plazo impuesto y la falta de avances concretos en las negociaciones. Las acciones estadounidenses mantienen un sesgo alcista, aunque con limitaciones técnicas para superar niveles máximos previos a la posible escalada militar. Sectores defensivos y aquellos vinculados a la energía y defensa se posicionan como refugios relativos, mientras que la aversión al riesgo modera la toma de posiciones más agresivas. En conjunto, la reacción del mercado evidencia una mezcla de optimismo contenido y prudencia ante la posibilidad de un desenlace abrupto.

Desde una perspectiva institucional, la lectura es que la administración estadounidense, pese a la retórica belicosa, evalúa con cautela los costos de una escalada militar significativa, lo que sugiere una preferencia por buscar una salida negociada o al menos un aplazamiento del plazo. El capital se está posicionando de forma selectiva, privilegiando activos que se benefician de la tensión geopolítica y evitando exposiciones que puedan sufrir un impacto directo en caso de conflicto abierto. La persistencia de esta crisis prolonga las presiones inflacionarias y las disrupciones en las cadenas de suministro, manteniendo un entorno macroeconómico desafiante que condiciona las decisiones de inversión a nivel global.

Los próximos eventos clave que pueden validar o modificar esta narrativa incluyen el cumplimiento o incumplimiento del plazo del 7 de abril, que será un punto de inflexión para la dirección del conflicto. La publicación de las minutas del FOMC el 8 de abril aportará claridad sobre la percepción del banco central respecto a los riesgos geopolíticos e inflacionarios. Además, las decisiones de política monetaria en Nueva Zelanda y los reportes económicos en EE.UU. y Europa durante la semana influirán en la sensibilidad del mercado. La evolución de las negociaciones mediadas por Pakistán, Egipto y Turquía, así como la elección especial en Georgia, también serán indicadores relevantes para anticipar cambios en la dinámica política y su impacto en la crisis.

vital.source_subject: Vital Knowledge: Vital Dawn for Tuesday April 7, 2026
vital.market_levels:
Market levels – the US equity futures are flat-to-up small so far this morning: S&P +3.2 points/5bp, Dow +62 points/13bp, Nasdaq flat, and R2 K flat. The major Eurozone indices are rallying 65-75bp, and the gains are broad. Areas of outperformance include luxury, basic resources, chemicals, staples, banks, insurance, media, retail, and telecoms while industrials, tech, real estate, healthcare, energy, and utilities underperform . UMG is the top stock in the SXXP on back of the Bill Ackman bid (other top names include WPP, STM, Norsk Hydro, and Yara ). ASML is sliding on the US bill (introduced Thurs night) imposing fresh restrictions on semi equipment sales to China. Stocks in Asia generally saw gains on Tuesday: TPX +0.25%, NKY +0.03%, SHCOMP +0.26%, Shenzhen +0.8%, Taiwan TAIEX +2.02%, Korea KOSPI +0.82%, Australia ASX 200 +1.74%, and India is up ~15bp. Hong Kong was closed. Samsung Electronics opened higher on back of its blow-out Q1 results, but the stock wound up fading, finishing down ~70bp. Treasuries aren’t doing much (yields are flat-to-down 1bp), and Fed expectations are fairly steady too. The DXY is sliding 20bp. Brent is ticking down 35bp (to $109.40). Gold is rallying 90bp while silver ticks up 20bp. Bitcoin is dipping 100bp (to ~$69 K).
•
vital.whats_happening:
What’s happening this morning – it was fairly quiet on the Iran news front since the US close, although a WSJ article (which was published around 9pm ET Mon night) is receiving some attention. The WSJ report claimed that hopes are “fading” that a final deal can be reached by tonight’s 8pm ET deadline. We agree with that sentiment – a full and final agreement won’t be in place by tonight . However, since the options facing Trump are so bad , since the US has achieved the bulk of its strategic objectives , since negotiations are still ongoing , and since Iran has made some (small) concessions on Hormuz (the Iraq news from the weekend was meaningful), it’s likely that the Pentagon and IDF don’t commence a scorched search campaign against civilian infrastructure targets later today . Away from geopolitics, there were a few company/sector-specific developments out since the Mon close, including blow-out preliminary Q1 results from Samsung , a new TPU agreement between Broadcom and Google (and between Broadcom, Google, and Anthropic), favorable Medicare news for health insurers , and a Bill Ackman takeout bid for Universal Music Group .
•
vital.watching_today:
What we’ll be watching on Tuesday – the calendar is fairly sparse , although LEVI will report earnings after the close and Trump’s latest Iran deadline is 8pm ET (Trump on Mon insisted he won’t change the 4/7 deadline , but it could easily shift once again). In addition, a special House election in Georgia (for Marjorie Taylor Greene’s old seat) will be watched for insight into the Nov mid-terms (the Republican is widely expected to win, but a narrow margin of victory could point to trouble for the GOP later in the year).
•
vital.watching_next:
What we’ll be watching on Wednesday – the focus will be on Delta’s Q1 earnings report before the open, the FOMC minutes at 2pm ET, and a handful of earnings reports after the close ( APLD and STZ ).
•
vital.thinking_about_markets:
How we’re thinking about markets - notwithstanding his increasingly bellicose rhetoric, none of the escalatory options available to Trump (civilian infrastructure bombing campaign, militarily reopening Hormuz, and/or seizing the enriched uranium) are good ones and given the huge costs involved in each (along with the fact that the US strategic objectives have been achieved ) , he will be forced to pursue an offramp of some sort . Whether an actual ceasefire deal is achieved remains to be seen, but a significant escalation (especially one involving thousands of US boots on the ground) still seems unlikely , which means the SPX risks are to the upside ( although since 6900-7000 was a firm ceiling before the war, that will be an increasingly difficult threshold to surpass. Even if the fighting fully ends tonight, there is a significant stagflationary burst cascading through the global economy from the last ~5 weeks, the effects of which will be felt for many, many months to come).
•
vital.market_in_a_minute_macro:
Market in a Minute (macro) – hopes are fading for a deal to be reached between the US and Iran by tonight’s deadline ( WSJ ); Iran submitted a 10-point deal proposal to Washington, demanding a guarantee not to be attacked again, an end to the Lebanon war, and a termination of sanctions ( Trump called the Iranian blueprint a “significant step”, but “not good enough” ) ( NYT ); mediators from Pakistan, Egypt, and Turkey are working to broker an agreement (if Trump sees a deal coming together, he will likely push out the 4/7 8pm ET deadline ) ( Axios ); Pentagon is refining a list of civilian infrastructure targets for Trump to mitigate war crime charges should the White House proceed with a bombing campaign ( Politico ); Iran calls for human chains to be formed around power plants as the US civilian infrastructure deadline nears ( AP ); Iran’s Supreme Leader is said to be unconscious and receiving medical treatment , raising questions about who is actually running the country ( London Times ); China looks to wean its hog industry off American soy to save costs and secure food security ( Reuters )
•
vital.market_in_a_minute_micro_tuesday_morning:
Market in a Minute (micro – Tuesday morning) - AAPL (Apple) is encountering engineering and production setbacks for its new foldable i Phone, and this could lead to delays in the launch timeline ( Nikkei ); ASML shares tumble in EU trading after a bill was introduced Thurs night in the US that would ratchet up restrictions on semi equipment sales to China ( Reuters ); Space X aims to allocate a large chunk of its IPO for retail investors, and will host them for an analyst meeting on June 11 ahead of the offering ( Reuters ); Universal Music Group received a takeout bid from Bill Ackman worth more than $63 B, with the stock listing set to shift to NY upon completion of the transaction (under the terms of the deal, UMG would merge with Pershing Square Sparc Holdings, with shareholders receiving EU30.40/shr., including EU5.05/shr. in cash and 0.77 shares in the new entity) ( FT )
•
vital.market_in_a_minute_micro_monday_night:
Market in a Minute (micro – Monday night) – AVGO (Broadcom) deals point to upside risk on AI chip sales while Anthropic highlights ARR acceleration ( Vital Knowledge ); CASY (Casey’s General Stores) will replace HOLX (Hologic) in the S&P 500 prior to the opening of trading on Thurs 4/9; managed care stocks spike on updated Medicare rate proposal from CMS ( Vital Knowledge ); PSX (Phillips 66) said it would book a $900 MM mark-to-market loss on hedges due to the sharp recent rise in commodity prices; Samsung Electronics reported blow-out preliminary Q1 results ( Vital Knowledge )
•

Iran

• What bulls are saying : the escalatory options facing Trump are so bad (destroying Iran’s civilian infrastructure, militarily reopening Hormuz, and/or seizing the country’s enriched uranium) that none of them are likely , especially since the bulk of American strategic objectives have been achieved (the administration will be forced to seek out exit ramps lest it preside over a quagmire ). Hormuz isn’t completely shut , as multiple countries strike deals with Iran to ensure safe passage through the waterway ( Iraq was granted permission over the weekend , a significant development since that country was shipping more than 3 M BPD via Hormuz before the war). Trump sounded somewhat hopeful on Monday about the state of negotiations with Iran and expressed optimism that a deal can be reached before the deadline on Tues at 8pm ET. Iran submitted a 10-point deal proposal to Washington, demanding a guarantee not to be attacked again, an end to the Lebanon war, and a termination of sanctions (this was discussed in a NYT report ). According to Axios , mediators from Pakistan, Egypt, and Turkey are working to broker an agreement (if Trump sees a deal coming together, he will likely push out the 4/7 8pm ET deadline ).
• What bears are saying : the situation in Iran remains on the cusp of catastrophe as all the escalatory options available to Trump risk embroiling the Pentagon in a protracted ground campaign involving tens of thousands of troops and/or triggering a (further) massive economic fallout , with Tehran likely to intensify its campaign of terror against critical assets and infrastructure in the Middle East. Trump sounds very serious about launching an aggressive air campaign aimed at destroying Iran’s civil infrastructure , and media reports (including a WSJ article on Monday) continue to suggest this is a very real possibility . Iran rejected the idea of a 45-day ceasefire and demanded a permanent end to the war . According to a WSJ report Mon night, hopes are fading for a deal to be reached by the Tues night deadline . Deescalating is easier said than done with Iran’s ability and willingness to retaliate throughout the region proving more durable than anticipated while Israel’s war against Hezbollah in Lebanon intensifies and the Houthis become more involved (there are also questions about Iran’s remaining missile stockpiles – some reports suggest its arsenal and launching infrastructure remain substantial ). Even if the war completely ends tomorrow, the global economy will still be forced to grapple with a powerful and extended pulse of stagflationary pressure triggered by the multi-week stretch of fighting. Hormuz is never going back to the way it was before – a significant risk premium will be applied to all commodities and goods flowing through the waterway .
• Russia is providing intelligence and cyber assistance to Iran ( Reuters )
vital.us_macro_section:
US macro

• The services ISM for Mar (out Mon morning) was generally downbeat (and stagflationary) , with the headline index falling 2.1 points M/M to 54 (the Street was modeling 54.9) while many of the key sub-indices painted a gloomy portrait of growth ( business activity/production -6 points M/M, employment -6.6 points, backlog of orders -2.3 points, and new export orders -6.5 points) along with a scorching hot picture of inflation (the prices index surged 7.7 points to 70.7, the highest reading since Oct 2022).
• The services PMI for Mar (out Fri morning) was very weak , signaling that the US economy is “ buckling under the strain of rising prices and intensifying uncertainty ”. The US jobs report (out Fri morning) contained some red flags , including a continued dependence on healthcare for driving employment creation and negative Household Survey details (the number of employed people fell by 64 K, the participation rate dipped, and the civilian labor force size slumped by nearly 400 K people).
• The Fed is likely to be on the sidelines for (at least) the balance of the year when it comes to the Funds Rate, and the pace of asset purchases is set to fall sharply starting next month.
• Fed’s Hammack and Goolsbee warn that inflation is a much larger problem than employment ( Reuters )
• The NY Fed’s Global Supply Chain Pressure Index (which can be found here ) rose to the highest level in more than three years due to disruptions in the Middle East (although it is still well off its COVID highs).
vital.international_macro_section:
International macro

• Australia’s final services PMI for Mar came in at 46.3, down from the flash reading of 46.6 (Bloomberg)
• China looks to wean its hog industry off American soy to save costs and secure food security ( Reuters )
• ECB official warns of rising inflation risks in the Eurozone ( Reuters )
• Europe’s final services PMI for Mar came in at 50.2, up from the flash reading of 50.1 (Bloomberg)
• Japan household spending came in weaker than expected at -1.8% Y/Y (vs. the Street -0.8%) ( Bloomberg )
• Philippines CPI for Mar ran ahead of the Street at +4.1% (vs. the Street +3.8%) (Bloomberg)
• Sweden’s CPI cools more than anticipated in Mar, bolstering calls for the central bank to extend its rate pause ( Bloomberg )
• Taiwan’s opposition leader is set to arrive in China on Tues for what’s being billed as an “historic journey for peace” ( FT )
• Thailand’s CPI comes in a bit cooler than anticipated at +0.57% core for Mar (vs. the Street 0.7%) (Bloomberg)
• White House labeled erratic by erstwhile US allies around the world , with many wondering if they are even still partners w/America ( WSJ )

• Market in a Minute (macro) – hopes are fading for a deal to be reached between the US and Iran by tonight’s deadline ( WSJ ); Iran submitted a 10-point deal proposal to Washington, demanding a guarantee not to be attacked again, an end to the Lebanon war, and a termination of sanctions ( Trump called the Iranian blueprint a “significant step”, but “not good enough” ) ( NYT ); mediators from Pakistan, Egypt, and Turkey are working to broker an agreement (if Trump sees a deal coming together, he will likely push out the 4/7 8pm ET deadline ) ( Axios ); Pentagon is refining a list of civilian infrastructure targets for Trump to mitigate war crime charges should the White House proceed with a bombing campaign ( Politico ); Iran calls for human chains to be formed around power plants as the US civilian infrastructure deadline nears ( AP ); Iran’s Supreme Leader is said to be unconscious and receiving medical treatment , raising questions about who is actually running the country ( London Times ); China looks to wean its hog industry off American soy to save costs and secure food security ( Reuters )
• Market in a Minute (micro – Tuesday morning) - AAPL (Apple) is encountering engineering and production setbacks for its new foldable i Phone, and this could lead to delays in the launch timeline ( Nikkei ); ASML shares tumble in EU trading after a bill was introduced Thurs night in the US that would ratchet up restrictions on semi equipment sales to China ( Reuters ); Space X aims to allocate a large chunk of its IPO for retail investors, and will host them for an analyst meeting on June 11 ahead of the offering ( Reuters ); Universal Music Group received a takeout bid from Bill Ackman worth more than $63 B, with the stock listing set to shift to NY upon completion of the transaction (under the terms of the deal, UMG would merge with Pershing Square Sparc Holdings, with shareholders receiving EU30.40/shr., including EU5.05/shr. in cash and 0.77 shares in the new entity) ( FT )
• Market in a Minute (micro – Monday night) – AVGO (Broadcom) deals point to upside risk on AI chip sales while Anthropic highlights ARR acceleration ( Vital Knowledge ); CASY (Casey’s General Stores) will replace HOLX (Hologic) in the S&P 500 prior to the opening of trading on Thurs 4/9; managed care stocks spike on updated Medicare rate proposal from CMS ( Vital Knowledge ); PSX (Phillips 66) said it would book a $900 MM mark-to-market loss on hedges due to the sharp recent rise in commodity prices; Samsung Electronics reported blow-out preliminary Q1 results ( Vital Knowledge )
• Calendar for Tuesday 4/7 – the focus will be on US durable/capital goods orders for Feb (8:30am ET), NY Fed consumer expectations survey for Mar (11am ET), US consumer credit for Feb (3pm ET), earnings after the US close ( GBX, LEVI ), analyst meetings ( NTNX ), Japan’s real cash earnings for Feb (Tues night), and New Zealand’s rate decision (Tues night).
Iran

• What bulls are saying : the escalatory options facing Trump are so bad (destroying Iran’s civilian infrastructure, militarily reopening Hormuz, and/or seizing the country’s enriched uranium) that none of them are likely , especially since the bulk of American strategic objectives have been achieved (the administration will be forced to seek out exit ramps lest it preside over a quagmire ). Hormuz isn’t completely shut , as multiple countries strike deals with Iran to ensure safe passage through the waterway ( Iraq was granted permission over the weekend , a significant development since that country was shipping more than 3 M BPD via Hormuz before the war). Trump sounded somewhat hopeful on Monday about the state of negotiations with Iran and expressed optimism that a deal can be reached before the deadline on Tues at 8pm ET. Iran submitted a 10-point deal proposal to Washington, demanding a guarantee not to be attacked again, an end to the Lebanon war, and a termination of sanctions (this was discussed in a NYT report ). According to Axios , mediators from Pakistan, Egypt, and Turkey are working to broker an agreement (if Trump sees a deal coming together, he will likely push out the 4/7 8pm ET deadline ).
• What bears are saying : the situation in Iran remains on the cusp of catastrophe as all the escalatory options available to Trump risk embroiling the Pentagon in a protracted ground campaign involving tens of thousands of troops and/or triggering a (further) massive economic fallout , with Tehran likely to intensify its campaign of terror against critical assets and infrastructure in the Middle East. Trump sounds very serious about launching an aggressive air campaign aimed at destroying Iran’s civil infrastructure , and media reports (including a WSJ article on Monday) continue to suggest this is a very real possibility . Iran rejected the idea of a 45-day ceasefire and demanded a permanent end to the war . According to a WSJ report Mon night, hopes are fading for a deal to be reached by the Tues night deadline . Deescalating is easier said than done with Iran’s ability and willingness to retaliate throughout the region proving more durable than anticipated while Israel’s war against Hezbollah in Lebanon intensifies and the Houthis become more involved (there are also questions about Iran’s remaining missile stockpiles – some reports suggest its arsenal and launching infrastructure remain substantial ). Even if the war completely ends tomorrow, the global economy will still be forced to grapple with a powerful and extended pulse of stagflationary pressure triggered by the multi-week stretch of fighting. Hormuz is never going back to the way it was before – a significant risk premium will be applied to all commodities and goods flowing through the waterway .
• Russia is providing intelligence and cyber assistance to Iran ( Reuters )
US macro

• The services ISM for Mar (out Mon morning) was generally downbeat (and stagflationary) , with the headline index falling 2.1 points M/M to 54 (the Street was modeling 54.9) while many of the key sub-indices painted a gloomy portrait of growth ( business activity/production -6 points M/M, employment -6.6 points, backlog of orders -2.3 points, and new export orders -6.5 points) along with a scorching hot picture of inflation (the prices index surged 7.7 points to 70.7, the highest reading since Oct 2022).
• The services PMI for Mar (out Fri morning) was very weak , signaling that the US economy is “ buckling under the strain of rising prices and intensifying uncertainty ”. The US jobs report (out Fri morning) contained some red flags , including a continued dependence on
vital.tmt_section:
TMT

• AAPL (Apple) is encountering engineering and production setbacks for its new foldable i Phone, and this could lead to delays in the launch timeline ( Nikkei )
• AI model theft: Open AI, Anthropic , and Google will work together to prevent “adversarial distillation attempts” ( Bloomberg )
• AI is causing “code overload” whereby companies are drowning in a sea of surging lines of code ( NYT )
• AMZN (Amazon)/USPS – Amazon struck a delivery deal w/USPS that will see the postal service retain ~80% of its existing volume with the e-commerce giant, or >1 B packages annually ( Reuters )
• Anthropic said that demand from Claude customers has accelerated so far in 2026 , with ARR now north of $30 B (up from $9 B at the end of 2025). Back in February, Anthropic noted that more than 500 business customers were each spending $1 M in annualized revenue – that number is now above 1,000 .
• ASML shares tumble in EU trading after a bill was introduced Thurs night in the US that would ratchet up restrictions on semi equipment sales to China ( Reuters )
• AVGO (Broadcom) announced a long-term agreement to develop and support custom TPUs for Google , along with supplying networking and other components for Google’s AI racks , through 2031 (in addition, Google, Broadcom, and Anthropic expanded their strategic collaboration under which Anthropic, starting in 2027, will access approximately 3.5 GW of TPU-based compute capacity ). Bottom Line : these agreements point to upside risk to Broadcom’s prior guidance for >$100 B in 2027 AI revenue.
• Music & AI : the major labels have yet to reach a licensing deal w/AI music startup Suno , and talks have hit a stalemate ( FT )
• Open AI’s Sam Altman told Axios that AI superintelligence is “so close” and “so mind-bending” , and will be “so disruptive” ( Axios )
• Open AI’s Sam Altman was the subject of a long profile article in The New Yorker, and the tone of the report was negative, raising questions about his character and ethics ( The New Yorker )
• Samsung Electronics reported blow-out preliminary Q1 results , including sales +68% Y/Y to KRW133 T (vs. the Street KRW117 T) and op. profit up ~750% to KRW57.2 T (vs. the Street KRW39.3 T). Samsung doesn’t provide any detail on segment performance (that will come later in the month alongside the official Q1 release), but it’s very likely memory played a big role in the massive Y/Y increases for both revenue and (especially) profitability ( Vital Knowledge )
• Space X aims to allocate a large chunk of its IPO for retail investors , and will host them for an analyst meeting on June 11 ahead of the offering ( Reuters )
• Universal Music Group received a takeout bid from Bill Ackman worth more than $63 B , with the stock listing set to shift to NY upon completion of the transaction (under the terms of the deal, UMG would merge with Pershing Square Sparc Holdings, with shareholders receiving EU30.40/shr. , including EU5.05/shr. in cash and 0.77 shares in the new entity) ( FT )

reuters.body_excerpt:
And, TSA tips fuel over 800 ICE arrests.

Daily Briefing

Daily Briefing

By Anisha De

Hello. Iran defiant on eve of Trump's ceasefire deadline , tips from TSA fuel more ICE arrests than previously known and Space X lays out IPO details .

Plus, pictures of Earth and the moon from the Artemis II historic voyage.

Today's Top News

The rubble of a building of the Sharif University of Technology, damaged in a strike in Tehran, Iran, April 7, 2026. Majid Asgaripour/WANA (West Asia News Agency) via REUTERS

War in the Middle East

•

Iran and Israel traded attacks as Tehran refused to reopen the Strait of Hormuz and accept a ceasefire deal on the eve of a deadline set by US President Donald Trump ‌to agree to his demands or get "taken out."
•

Russian satellites have made dozens of detailed imagery surveys of military facilities and critical sites across the Middle East to help Iran strike US forces and other targets, according to a Ukrainian intelligence assessment. Read our exclusive report .
•

At least two attackers were killed and one was seriously injured in an extended gun ‌battle between police and assailants directly outside the building housing the Israeli consulate in Istanbul, according to media reports and Reuters video.

In other news

•

US Immigration and Customs Enforcement arrested more than 800 people following tips shared by federal airport security officials from the start of Trump's presidency through February 2026, internal ICE data reviewed by ‌Reuters show, a figure far above what was previously publicly known.
•

The White House is proposing to cut more than 9,400 workers and just over $1.5 billion from the 60,000-employee Transportation ‌Security Administration that handles airport security operations, according to budget documents.
•

Specialist mini jet engine makers across Europe are ramping up production and investment to head off a looming supply shortage that threatens to hold back Ukraine's rapid deep-strike drone programme at a critical stage of the war with Russia.
•

US Vice President JD Vance is traveling to Hungary on a mission to boost the campaign of nationalist Prime Minister Viktor Orban, who faces the toughest re-election bid of his career. Correspondent Anita Komuves joins the Reuters World News podcas t to explain why some global leaders are lining up behind him.

• Resident doctors in England started a six-day walkout after rejecting an offer the government said would not get better, with the British Medical Association saying it failed to reverse years of pay erosion and staffing pressures.
•

Australia's most decorated soldier, Ben Roberts-Smith, was arrested and charged with five counts of war crimes relating to the killing of unarmed civilians while on deployment in Afghanistan.

Business & Markets

Space X's logo and an Elon Musk photo are seen in this illustration created on December 19, 2022. REUTERS/Dado Ruvic/Illustration/File Photo

•

Space X outlined details of its highly anticipated IPO at a meeting with ‌its team of bankers, telling them it plans to earmark a large portion of shares for retail investors and will host 1,500 of them at an event in June following the IPO roadshow launch, according to two people familiar with the matter. However, the blockbuster listing could suck the oxygen out of the fragile US IPO market .

• Bill Ackman's Pershing Square proposed merging its acquisition vehicle ‌with Universal Music Group with a plan to list in the United States in a deal aimed at reviving the world's biggest music label's value.
•

Americans starting weight-loss medicines for the first time want lower cost and greater convenience as they consider pills from Novo Nordisk or Eli Lilly according to seven doctors who specialize in obesity. Read how the pill vs. shot debate is reshaping the weight loss drug market.
•

Air India CEO Campbell Wilson has resigned , a source with direct knowledge of ‌the matter said, as the airline grapples with persistent losses and heightened regulatory scrutiny following a crash last year that killed 260 people.
•

Brazil has put China's BYD on a ‌registry of employers who have subjected workers to conditions similar to slavery , after a 2024 scandal in which Chinese workers were said to have been victims of human trafficking and abusive contracts.
•

Samsung Electronics projected its first-quarter earnings would exceed its entire profit for last year , beating expectations as booming demand for artificial intelligence infrastructure stretched supply and drove chip prices higher.

filtered_signal.driver_principal: La incertidumbre geopolítica y militar en torno a la crisis entre Estados Unidos e Irán, con un inminente plazo impuesto por el presidente Trump para un acuerdo o una posible escalada militar, domina la percepción de riesgo y el comportamiento de los mercados globales.
hechos_confirmados: El presidente Trump estableció un plazo para el 7 de abril de 2026 a las 8pm ET para que Irán acepte sus demandas, incluyendo la reapertura del Estrecho de Hormuz y un acuerdo de alto el fuego.
Irán ha rechazado la reapertura del Estrecho de Hormuz y no ha aceptado un acuerdo de alto el fuego antes del plazo establecido.
Las negociaciones entre Estados Unidos e Irán continúan, con mediadores de Pakistán, Egipto y Turquía intentando facilitar un acuerdo.
Rusia está proporcionando apoyo de inteligencia y cibernético a Irán para ataques contra fuerzas estadounidenses y otros objetivos en Medio Oriente.
filtered_signal.escenario_dominante: Existe una alta probabilidad de que no se alcance un acuerdo definitivo antes del plazo del 7 de abril, pero una escalada militar significativa, especialmente una campaña de bombardeos masivos o un despliegue terrestre amplio de tropas estadounidenses, tiene una moderada probabilidad de ocurrir debido a los altos costos y objetivos estratégicos ya alcanzados por Estados Unidos. Los mercados probablemente mantendrán una volatilidad elevada con sesgo alcista en acciones estadounidenses, aunque con limitaciones técnicas en niveles máximos previos a la guerra.
filtered_signal.interpretacion_institucional: La situación geopolítica en Medio Oriente sigue siendo el principal factor de riesgo para los mercados globales, con la incertidumbre sobre la resolución del conflicto entre Estados Unidos e Irán generando cautela. Aunque la retórica de Trump es belicosa, las opciones de escalada militar son costosas y poco atractivas, lo que sugiere que la administración buscará una salida negociada o un aplazamiento del plazo. Los mercados reflejan esta dinámica con movimientos mixtos y selectivos, favoreciendo sectores defensivos y aquellos beneficiados por la tensión geopolítica, como energía y defensa. La persistencia de la crisis implica un impacto prolongado en la inflación global y en las cadenas de suministro, manteniendo un entorno macroeconómico desafiante.
riesgos_secundarios: Una escalada militar inesperada que incluya ataques masivos a infraestructura civil iraní o un despliegue terrestre amplio de tropas estadounidenses en la región.
Inestabilidad política interna en Irán debido al estado de salud del líder supremo, lo que podría complicar las negociaciones o aumentar la violencia regional.
Intensificación del conflicto entre Israel y grupos aliados de Irán, como Hezbollah, que podría ampliar el conflicto regional.
Retrasos o cambios en el plazo impuesto por Trump, que podrían generar incertidumbre adicional en los mercados.
Impactos económicos y sociales derivados de una prolongada crisis energética y de suministro global, especialmente en commodities y sectores sensibles a la inflación.
eventos_clave: Plazo final impuesto por Trump para un acuerdo con Irán el 7 de abril de 2026 a las 8pm ET.
Publicación de las minutas de la reunión del FOMC el 8 de abril de 2026, que puede reflejar la percepción del banco central sobre riesgos geopolíticos e inflación.
Decisión de política monetaria de Nueva Zelanda y reportes económicos clave en EE.UU. y Europa durante la semana, que pueden influir en la reacción de los mercados ante la crisis.
Elección especial en Georgia que puede ofrecer señales sobre el clima político en EE.UU. y su posible impacto en la política exterior.
Evolución de las negociaciones mediadas por Pakistán, Egipto y Turquía para un posible acuerdo entre EE.UU. e Irán.
